summary:
The Ertebølle culture, named after a shell midden site in Jutland, Denmark, was a late Mesolithic hunter-gatherer-fisher culture of Scandinavia and the western Baltic region, with origins dating to approximately 6400 BCE. It developed from and succeeded the [[event:1484|Kongemose culture]] in the same region and is recognized as one of the most successful and culturally elaborate hunter-gatherer traditions in European prehistory, persisting until approximately 3900 BCE — a remarkable duration that saw the culture resist or slowly absorb farming practices while maintaining a predominantly foraging economy.

The Ertebølle people were masterful exploiters of the coastal, estuarine, and forest environments of the western Baltic. Their large shell middens — accumulations of discarded mollusk shells, fish bones, and other debris — attest to intensive and sustained shellfish harvesting from the rich Littrina Sea coastlines. Beyond mollusks, Ertebølle communities hunted red deer, wild boar, aurochs, and sea mammals, and fished intensively using nets, fish traps, and lines. This diverse economy supported relatively dense and sedentary populations for a non-farming society, with communities establishing long-term base camps that generated the deep stratified middens now found across the Danish coast.

Significantly, Ertebølle communities adopted pottery — primarily pointed-base cooking vessels suited to being propped over fire — independently or through contact with farming communities, without adopting agriculture. This demonstrates that ceramic technology could spread independently of the farming complex, challenging the traditional equation of pottery with the Neolithic agricultural package. The eventual Ertebølle transition to farming in the late fourth millennium BCE, when the culture transformed into the Funnel Beaker culture, represents a late and distinctive Neolithization of Scandinavia.
